sentences.text: From the-- from the occupied territories, from the Russian occupied?
This was in January of 1941, if I'm-- no, yeah, 1940.
You see because, yeah, then I was-- '39-- but this was in winter time.
Yes.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] People were afraid to go out in the street, because under-- the German were there.
We had casualties that took out-- I-- we knew-- they took out some people and they shot them, because they were doing some business.
You know-- their business.
What business?
He bought something from them, and they he sold it to them, and they sold it to them.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]
I went there, and I was looking for some work.
I couldn't find.
I had to do the same thing like some other people.
I bought from them something, and I sold to them.
And sold-- this was the way how we tried to manage ourselves and to keep up ourselves.
And I-- I got married.
When did you get married?
I got married in '42.
[PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] Beginning of '42.
And then I got a child.
I have a child, a little boy.
Shloime.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And we were with-- I was together with my wife's father and mother.
They had six children.
There were six children also.
But we tried to manage to be together.
You know?
And then, I moved out from there-- in the ghetto.
I moved in the-- yeah.
The ghetto was formed in '42, was the ghetto ready.
Yes, '42. by the end of '41-- by the end of '41 to '42 was the ghetto.
What?
Yes, there was a judenrat.
Yes.
I have it here.
I have the book.
And I have the people from the judenrat in that book and from the Otwock.
I knew it.
I cannot recall the names.
I knew their names.
There was a judenrat, was police, was everything.
That's what the Germans wanted, and they got it.
And they start to take out-- a lot of young people.
They took out-- they send them away to Lublin-- somewhere, Lublin, some other places to work there.
In the beginning, not.
In the beginning was not closed.
Certain places let's say you have to live there or you couldn't live there.
Then when they start to put it together, they close it up together that you couldn't-- that you have to move in.
And that-- so it separates place, and to live there.
They close it-- let's say that was-- if they close it up this place, they put up a barrier.
They say you couldn't go out from that barrier.
You-- there was quite a few barriers there on that place that say that from this side, and the other side.
So you couldn't be-- I have to live only in this.
I'd been in the other-- you see, There were two places.
There was this side that has-- that is more on the Jewish section, where the Jews were living before the war.
And there was the other side.
There were the hotels, and the more comfortable people that were living in the other side, and the hospital there that was there.
So I-- so from one place to the other, you could go out.
You see, you could come in from this ghetto, from this place to the other place.
You couldn't go out from that-- from that place.
To go out from that place was very hard.
You couldn't go out from that place.
Were there guards?
Guards, there was.
Then they informed the Jewish police.
And that was the Jewish police were standing guard there.
And they didn't let out.
To go out, more the Aryan side.
No, they didn't have any special documents.
They gave out the-- the arm bands.
You had to wear an arm band, you know?
The arm band was a white arm band with a Jewish star on the arm band.
We had white.
The Jewish star was yellow.
On white, yes.
What?
No, no, nothing.
No.
The law came out this-- only one about-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] end of-- the beginning of '42.
Everybody.
Oh, from boys from the, let's say, from 12, 13 years old.
But children not.
Everybody-- everyone has to wear their arm band.
These people where they had their businesses and their more-- thought they had to go out from there.
They said they have to come in to the ghetto.
They were in the ghetto.
And the whole business consistent at that time between me and you, you and me.
You know-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] it's an exchange.
He gave you potatoes-- they gave him some other things.
And they gave him this.
The Poles, they came in once in a while, you see, and they sold it.
You see, we had to--
There was not enough food.
This is-- I had at that time that little boy, which we were looking for-- carrots, squeeze the carrots to have-- to give him a little juice.
It was terrible.
Until the-- they started to liquidate us in 1943.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But they-- but I end up-- that's-- only-- I'm mixed up again with the time.
[PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS]
I'm talking about the ghetto, how they closed up the ghetto.
And the people that was in the ghetto and not having every day, every single day we had funerals, and funerals.
Moshe's died.
They had died.
They had died.
We had funerals constantly there.
We had to take them out.
And the ghetto was-- they started in 1942-- the liquidation from that ghetto started in 1942.

They came in and we heard-- you know, some other places-- from some other places for how they-- they are handling everything that they can get together-- the people, they took them out to the trains, to the cattle cars.
And this was an early morning.
In a fall-- September.
Before the holidays.
This was somewhere around September.
We heard the ghettos full with-- you know-- with Germans.
And Ukrainians came into the ghetto, and started to take out people.
And they made there like a little Umschlagplatz right by this station there.
And they--they fooled them.
They said, you're going to work.
They asked the women to take some-- some powder, some salt, because you're going to wash the clothing.
And everybody grabbed for whatever they can and then they went out, you see.
And they took them over to that place where they have the-- they took them together at that station, in the [NON-ENGLISH] station.
And they start to load them up to the-- I was remaining in that ghetto.
I was there in the ghetto.
I was hiding myself.
And the-- the woman what I was with her, she was in a separate, but I didn't know where she is.
And she didn't know where I am.
Finally, we found ourselves.
And the child was with her parents.
And they took care of him quite a few days, the first few days after the liquidation from there-- and they take care.
Then we take the child out from there.
And she said, the woman-- wife, at that time, I'm going to go.
I'm going to take-- as a Polish woman, I will try to go to [NON-ENGLISH] We heard that in [NON-ENGLISH], they have some more freedom.
They live better.
And they can go into work.
So she said-- we-- she hired somebody, we hired-- we hired a woman, a Polish woman.
She should keep her company, and go in with her to [NON-ENGLISH], she should be in [NON-ENGLISH], she should live there.

And meanwhile, this woman what we hired, she was not interested in her life, either on that child's life-- she was more interesting to see to go into that house where she lived and take out from there everything.
She said, you stay here, and I'm going to-- and a day later, she didn't-- they were hiding, and they couldn't get out because they need something to do.
Near Otwock was a camp, a working camp, which they had that organized before the-- the liquidation from that ghetto.
And they were working there on that camp.

When the-- my wife's father said, look, I have some friends that maybe we going to go to that camp, maybe they will be able to get into that camp and stay there until that's the time to regulate a little bit.
And I was there.
I was in ghetto.
I remained in ghetto with some other boys.
We were hiding ourselves.
We were-- you know, if there were Germans, we know-- we were young men, you know?
I could run around.
And my-- and she was-- and she went with her father, and father with another sister.
And she went to that camp.
And the whole-- the other children, they went over to that-- to that-- to that little Umschlagplatz.
You know, where they call that Umschlagplatz?
And they were transferred to Treblinka.
We knew-- then we found out that they're going to Treblinka, they send them to Treblinka to liquidation.
That's Karczew.
No.
No, I don't know it.
We give her-- money we didn't have.
We gave her certain things from the house-- from the housework.
She had a machine, you know, a sewing machine, and some other thing-- you know, utensils.
And she took it and that's all.
Although she took-- she did-- she left them out, you know?
And then my wife, her father and with child with another sister, they went to Karczew, you know, to that camp.
They wanted come into the camp there.
They couldn't get into that camp.
It was impossible.
They should be able to go into the camp.
And they were--about a day, two days they were outside the camp.
You know.
And then finally, the Germans with Ukrainian, they-- they caught them.
And they took them to a place, and they shot them.
The baby was 10, 11 months old-- was shot with her mother, her father.
That picture that I have here, this is-- this is a grave, which was 19 people was there buried.
Now that's what I'm going to come to it after the liberations, when I found out her brother, my wife, all of us also there, and we went to that place and we exhumed them.
And put them in coffins.
And we took them to the Jewish cemetery.
Over there, they were shot.
They couldn't get into that camp, and they were shot them outside the camp.
Before them they-- they took out just the young boys to work.
But there was now a liquidation that said that police, the-- the-- the judenrat had to give them a certain amount of people in this and this time.
If not, they have to pay some contra-- some contraband for the Germans, if they would not give them to the amount of people.
They tried to get it.
They catch the young boys-- and the police.
And they send them away to somewhere in Lublin.
The liquidation was one time, that's right.
Liquidation was one time.
And I remained there in the ghetto.
After the liquidation, I remained in the ghetto.
We had some people came back, and they heard it.
We didn't know it.
We start to hear about Treblinka that time.
We heard from before then some people they are taking the Treblinka, and the Treblinka is the place where they are liquidate them.
Don't believe in anything.
You're not going to the east to work, because it's not a working place.
This is just a liquidation.

And as a matter of fact, that first group, which they took away from Otwock, they took into Treblinka and they selected some of the more-- the carpenters, the [INAUDIBLE], and they start to build another-- another part of Treblinka, which they were there.
And from them, you know, we found it out, because there was then there is another Treblinka where they burned the people, they killed the people.
And they said, the other Treblinka.
Don't get-- be fooled, they said, because we are working in this.
And they are-- I knew some people, when I got there, which they built that Treblinka.
We couldn't imagine ourselves.
Then they're taking people.
Now then, I started to believe, because I lived-- I knew the people which there were there but in [NON-ENGLISH], but in camp, they grab them, they caught them, and they shot them.
So I say, if they can shot them here, they most probably it's true what they're doing there.
Let's say, in some people in the beginning, they escaped from that.
It was not so guarded, then they should not be able to escape.
Then they came back to the ghettos.
And they told us.
And nobody couldn't believe it.
You're taking some people, and you burn them.
You put them in fire and burn them.
You gas them in gas chambers.
They didn't believe it, you see?
And I was there in that ghetto.
And I was the same thing like some other one.
How could they do something like this?
I couldn't understand.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
We was a group of boys in that ghetto.
We hide-- after the liquidation from ghetto, we start to come out.
And they took the oldest-- the remnants from that ghetto, the young ones, more healthier ones, and they took them out, and they send them to Warsaw in that camp.
And I was there, Tarchomin.
The camp, the name of the camp was Tarchomin.
We were sent there about 200 people, not just from the Otwock.
They came from some other, from the-- from the other places, also around in the Tarchomin.
And we went to Tarchomin.
And we did some work.
And we dig there-- all kinds of work.
Every day, every two, three days there was an assessment which used to come to Otwock, also.
They called him [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH] was his name.
And he-- his pleasure was he had to kill some people.
He was-- kill-- They took out everyday some people and he killed them there in Tarchomin.
Until, you know, the-- we were came from the 200, we became 175, less, less, less.
And then, they started to liquidate all the side camps, let's say, that Karczew, and that Tarchomin, and there's some other they started liquidate.
And they pushed them into the ghetto to--
To Warsaw.
Yeah, because-- in Tarchomin, I could go about 15 minutes I was in the center of the town from Warsaw, you see?
I could be there by taking the-- the train, a railroad train.
And we go in there.
Then when they liquidate-- start to liquidate Tarchomin, I've been there in Tarchomin, they came, took out our group.
The rest they shot at on that place.
And we went-- and they took us into the ghetto to Warsaw.
This was in 19-- 1942.
Just looked at their faces.
Come, come, come.
That's not-- this [NON-ENGLISH] that's-- you know, whoever was hiding in the barracks as he was hiding.
He didn't see it.
Took about an hour.
You know, they came for a visit, just for a visit to kill them.
To Warsaw we were transferred about 120 people, I would say.
All men, yeah.
No woman.
Oh, about 25, 26, to 30. 30 was the top of them.
We walked, from Tarchomin we walked.
It was not too far.
Were you were under guard when you walked?
Yeah, under guard, certainly.
Yeah, under  guard.
We came into Warsaw, in Warsaw, let us free.
They came and they-- in the city-- they let us free.
And we walked.
And-- then we started to look for somewhere where to go to live.
So that was in the ghetto?
That's was-- we were already in the ghetto.
And the people in the ghetto, it was open.
It was the ghetto, but it was still open.
It's not so strict, you see?
There was a time later when it was strict.
You couldn't even show your face out there in the ghetto, because if they saw during the day somebody, they shot him.
It was not-- you have to-- I have to do something.
Though in the beginning, it was a little free.
We asked the people, what-- what we do, and what we got to do?
And they said don't worry.
You're going to do the same thing what we are doing-- you see, waiting for the death.
Could you describe the situation in the Warsaw ghetto when you got there at the end of 1942?
When I got there, there was hunger.
Hunger.
Nobody can help.
They tried.
They saw we are coming from a camp somewhere, because we were marching together.
But they could not help us.
They did not have by themself, or-- to go into a store to buy something.
Everything was closed.
It was-- it was already the-- the-- the ghetto was closed.
And built up the-- the--
The wall was-- you say, we came in from the other side, and you couldn't get out anymore.
And being there, we tried.
We were a group of boys together.
Let's say-- I wasn't by myself.
We kept ourselves together, four or five boys.
And said, look, we have-- we have to do something to find a place where to live.
And what we're going to do, we're going to find out the next day.
We found a place and we-- in [NON-ENGLISH] 31, I still remember the number from that [NON-ENGLISH] 31 when we came in.
Because apartments was empty.
There was taken out people who that took them out from before that was empty.
But we went into an apartment, it was there.
They lived there in their apartment.
And we went in the morning to say-- you go out, some of the people-- we asked people, which there were in the ghetto.
You have to combine yourself.
You have to combine yourself with groups, and go out to work.
So, we-- we went into a group, you know, this is the [NON-ENGLISH] group.
So they took us to an older apartment.
They take out the furnitures and the stuffs and everything.
A lot boarded up in the trucks, when they-- the German trucks.
And they took it away.
Where they took it, I don't know.
I don't want to know at that time.
Was [NON-ENGLISH], was a [NON-ENGLISH].
Was one from the group, he was the leader of us, and he took us there.
We connect ourselves with that group, just to connect ourselves.
Because you had to go to work.
You-- you-- either you have to lay down, hidden the whole day, either you have to go out.
And by going out, you see, you could have the possibility to buy something, to bring in something to the ghetto, and you know to-- to keep up yourself.
No, no.
By buying from one to the other-- that's all right.
By buying and going-- and we were standing and selling these-- these flowers, these potatoes, this bread-- it was bakery.
And- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and that baking and selling some stores was-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And I've been there, with that group.
Then we went out to work, and we lived together.
You know-- then it came in that they-- a genuine action and the liquidation.
